text:
COMBINED TECHNETIUM-99m MDP BONE SCINTIGRAPHY AND CT OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant and known osseous and nodal metastases. Currently on olaparib 200 mg twice daily (dose-reduced from 300 mg BID due to Grade 3 anemia approximately 6 weeks prior) plus ongoing androgen deprivation therapy (leuprolide). Denosumab therapy ongoing for bone protection. Restaging following biochemical response to olaparib (PSA decline from 8.3 ng/mL at initiation to nadir of 2.8 ng/mL most recently). Prior treatment history includes ADT with abiraterone/prednisone (discontinued after approximately 24 months due to castration-resistant progression) and enzalutamide (discontinued after approximately 6 months due to biochemical and radiographic progression). Comparison with prior PSMA PET/CT performed at time of enzalutamide progression and prior bone scan/CT performed approximately 7 months ago.

TECHNIQUE:
Bone scintigraphy: 25 mCi Tc-99m methylene diphosphonate (MDP) injected intravenously. Whole-body planar images obtained in anterior and posterior projections at approximately 3 hours post-injection. SPECT/CT acquisition performed through the thoracolumbar spine and pelvis.

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Volumetric CT acquisition performed from the lung apices through the pubic symphysis following administration of 80 mL intravenous iohexol contrast (rate 2.5 mL/sec). Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformatted images generated. Oral contrast was not administered.

---

BONE SCAN FINDINGS:

SKELETON — AXIAL:
Thoracic spine: The previously identified focus of increased radiotracer activity at the T10 vertebral body (first identified on PSMA PET/CT at the time of enzalutamide progression) demonstrates noticeably diminished intensity of uptake compared with prior bone scintigraphy. The lesion retains mildly above-background activity but has improved substantially from prior. Correlative SPECT/CT imaging demonstrates a corresponding sclerotic focus within the T10 vertebral body, with the degree of sclerosis appearing slightly increased from prior, in keeping with treatment-induced osteoblastic remodeling.

Lumbar spine: The previously described lesions at L3 and L4 continue to demonstrate mildly increased uptake, however both show reduced photon intensity compared with the most recently available bone scan. On SPECT/CT, both lesions appear predominantly sclerotic, a pattern most consistent with ongoing reparative response. L1 and L2 vertebral bodies show no abnormal areas of tracer concentration.

Sacrum: The previously documented focus of uptake at the right sacral ala has decreased markedly in activity and is now only faintly discernible above background. No new sacral lesions identified.

SKELETON — APPENDICULAR:
Ribs: The previously identified foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake distributed across multiple ribs (right posterior 7th rib, and multiple left-sided posterior rib lesions identified at time of enzalutamide progression) are markedly less conspicuous on the current examination. The right posterior 7th rib focus has decreased to near-background levels. Left-sided posterior rib lesions, which numbered approximately four to five sites on prior imaging, now show only two areas with faint, mildly elevated activity — the remainder have essentially resolved to background. No new rib lesions are identified.

Pelvis — right iliac wing: The right iliac wing focus shows reduced tracer avidity compared with prior, with SPECT/CT confirming a predominantly sclerotic morphology. Right femoral neck: the longstanding lesion at the right femoral neck (present since initial staging) retains low-level activity on the current study, similar to the most recent prior exam, without clear interval change — this site appears to be in a plateau of activity consistent with mature sclerotic remodeling.

Pelvis — bilateral iliac crests and left pelvic structures: The bilateral iliac crest lesions first identified at the time of enzalutamide progression show considerably diminished radiotracer uptake bilaterally compared with prior, with no new pelvic osseous lesions identified.

Remaining skeleton: The skull, bilateral shoulder girdles, sternum, remaining thoracic and lumbar vertebral bodies, upper extremities, and bilateral lower extremities do not demonstrate any new focal abnormalities. No new sites of pathologic tracer concentration are identified.

Bone scan overall impression: Compared with prior, there is widespread improvement in the intensity of radiotracer uptake at all previously identified osseous metastatic sites. No new osseous lesions are seen. The pattern is most consistent with a meaningful treatment response to olaparib at the osseous metastatic sites.

---

CT CHEST FINDINGS:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or mass lesion is identified. No pulmonary nodules are seen. The central and segmental airways are patent. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinal and hilar contours are unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The heart size is within normal limits. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber with mild calcific atherosclerosis, appropriate for patient age.

Chest wall and soft tissues: No suspicious soft tissue lesion in the chest wall. Gynecomastia is present bilaterally, consistent with ongoing androgen deprivation therapy. No axillary lymphadenopathy.

Bone windows (thoracic): Redemonstration of sclerotic changes at T10, right posterior 7th rib, and left-sided posterior rib lesions, as described above on bone scan correlation. No cortical destruction or pathologic fracture identified in the thoracic skeleton. No new lytic or suspicious osseous lesion visualized in the chest.

---

CT ABDOMEN FINDINGS:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with a smooth hepatic contour. The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous on both portal venous and delayed phase images. No discrete focal hepatic lesion is identified. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein is patent.

Gallbladder and biliary system: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No cholelithiasis. Common bile duct is within normal caliber.

Pancreas: The pancreas demonstrates age-appropriate parenchymal atrophy without focal mass, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology, without discrete nodule or enlargement. Note is made that the patient has been on prior abiraterone acetate; no imaging features of adrenal insufficiency.

Kidneys and ureters: Both kidneys are normal in size and show symmetric contrast enhancement with prompt excretion. A 5 mm non-enhancing hypodense cortical cyst is identified in the upper pole of the right kidney, unchanged from prior. No hydronephrosis. No obstructing lesion.

Stomach and bowel: No bowel obstruction. The stomach, small bowel, and visualized large bowel appear unremarkable without wall thickening or mesenteric stranding.

Retroperitoneum and lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes. Aorta and great vessels are normal in caliber with mild atherosclerotic calcification.

Abdominal wall and soft tissues: No hernia or other abdominal wall abnormality. No free fluid.

Bone windows (upper abdomen/lumbar spine): Sclerotic changes consistent with prior osseous metastatic involvement at L3 and L4 are redemonstrated. No new abdominal osseous lesion identified.

---

CT PELVIS FINDINGS:

Lymph nodes: The right obturator lymph node, which was enlarged to 2.1 cm at time of enzalutamide progression and measured 1.4 cm on restaging at the outset of olaparib, now measures 1.2 cm in short axis. This represents continued interval decrease in size, consistent with ongoing treatment response. The nodal morphology is now more ovoid with a partially preserved fatty hilum. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the external iliac, internal iliac, or inguinal chains.

Prostate and seminal vesicles: Given the patient's prior diagnosis, the prostate bed is visualized and does not demonstrate discrete mass or lymphadenopathy. The seminal vesicle region appears post-treatment without dominant soft tissue abnormality.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially filled and unremarkable without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defect.

Rectum and sigmoid colon: No colonic mass or wall thickening. No perirectal lymphadenopathy.

Pelvic soft tissues: No pelvic free fluid. No soft tissue mass.

Bone windows (pelvis): Sclerotic changes in the right iliac wing, right femoral neck, right sacral ala, and bilateral iliac crests are redemonstrated, appearing stable to very mildly improved in sclerotic density. No new lytic or suspicious focal osseous lesion is identified in the pelvis.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Widespread improvement in the intensity of radiotracer uptake at all previously documented osseous metastatic sites, including the thoracic and lumbar spine (T10, L3, L4), sacrum, right iliac wing, right femoral neck, bilateral iliac crests, and multiple bilateral rib lesions. No new osseous lesions are identified. The constellation of findings — diminishing scintigraphic avidity with increasing sclerotic density on CT — is most consistent with a meaningful osseous treatment response to olaparib.

2. Continued interval decrease in size of the right obturator lymph node (now 1.2 cm in short axis, down from 1.4 cm at initiation of olaparib and 2.1 cm at time of enzalutamide progression), consistent with nodal treatment response.

3. No hepatic metastases. No pulmonary nodules or parenchymal metastatic disease. No new visceral involvement.

4. No new sites of metastatic disease identified in any compartment.

5. Gynecomastia, consistent with ongoing androgen deprivation therapy.

6. Stable small simple right renal cortical cyst.

Overall, the imaging findings across all modalities are consistent with a partial treatment response to olaparib across osseous and nodal disease sites, with no evidence of visceral metastatic disease on the current examination.
